edge: The goal in neuroscience has been to elucidate the functional organization of the brain.
Functional organization within the higher visual cortex has remained relatively coarse.
Functional accounts in the higher visual cortex focus on regions of interest and selectivity for broad categories.
Functional accounts include selectivity for categories such as faces.
Functional accounts include selectivity for categories such as places.
Functional accounts include selectivity for categories such as bodies.
Functional accounts include selectivity for categories such as food.
Functional accounts include selectivity for categories such as words.
The identification of regions of interest has relied on manually assembled stimulus sets.
To explore functional organization without robust hypotheses, a data-driven approach is introduced.
The approach Brain Diffusion for Visual Exploration (BrainDiVE) synthesizes images predicted to activate a given brain region using natural images and fMRI recordings.
BrainDiVE is able to synthesize preferred images with semantic specificity for category-selective ROIs.
BrainDiVE builds on generative methods by combining diffusion models with brain-guided image synthesis.
BrainDiVE can characterize differences between ROIs selective for the same high-level category and identify novel functional subdivisions within these ROIs.
Novel functional subdivisions within the ROIs are validated with behavioral data.
The results advance our understanding of the fine-grained functional organization of the brain.
The behavioral data helps advance our understanding of the fine-grained functional organization of human visual cortex.
The human visual cortex plays a fundamental role in our ability to process, interpret, and act on visual information.
Previous studies have provided important evidence that regions in the higher visual cortex preferentially process complex semantic categories.
Hand-selected, synthetic stimuli may bias the results of studies on the higher visual cortex.
Data-driven methods for interpreting high-dimensional neural tuning are complementary to traditional researcher-crafted stimulus approaches.
BrainDiVE is a generative approach for synthesizing images that are predicted to activate a given region in the human visual cortex.
BrainDiVE uses brain signals recorded via fMRI to guide the synthesis of images with high semantic specificity.
BrainDiVE leverages state-of-the-art diffusion models in identifying fine-grained functional specialization in an objective and data-driven manner.
Brain signals recorded via fMRI guide the synthesis of images with high semantic specificity.
Image diffusion models are typically driven by text prompts, but BrainDiVE replaces these prompts with maximization of voxels in given brain areas.
Synthesized images are tailored to targeted regions in higher-order visual areas
Analysis of synthesized images enables data-driven exploration of the underlying feature preferences for different visual cortical sub-regions
The synthesized images emphasize and isolate critical feature preferences
Synthesized images are optimized to maximize response of a given sub-region beyond what was present in the original stimulus images
Human behavioral studies were performed to validate the findings regarding synthesized images
BrainDiVE can synthesize novel images for category-selective brain regions with high semantic specificity
BrainDiVE can identify ROI-wise differences in selectivity mapping to relevant properties
BrainDiVE can enable new insights into the fine-grained functional organization of the human visual cortex

BrainDiVE can elucidate the functional properties of human cortical populations, making it a promising tool for exploratory neuroscience.
